purpose: Assesses whether private credit functions as a shock absorber or amplifier conditional on the type and speed of stress
components: Slow-burn stress regime: locked-up capital absorbs losses, suppresses volatility transmission, likely absorbed
Sudden liquidity event regime: $300B+ semi-liquid vehicles with quarterly redemptions, mark-to-model opacity, likely amplified
NAV autocorrelation of 0.5-0.7 suppresses measured volatility by 2-3x
Secondary market NAV discount trends as potential early warning indicator
Historical analogue: pre-2008 SIVs as partial but imperfect comparison
current_assessment: The binary framing (absorber vs amplifier) was refuted. The answer is regime-dependent: opacity absorbs slow-burn stress but may amplify sudden liquidity events. The $300B+ semi-liquid tranche is the fault line. Private credit could be the locus of surprise in the next downturn because risk is less visible, not necessarily larger.
limitations: No clean historical analogue for current private credit structure
NAV-based early warning indicators are conceptual and not backtested
Semi-liquid vehicle behavior under stress is entirely untested
Opacity means the regime transition from absorbed to amplified may not be observable in advance
